The TRX file still wasn't generated. With Microsoft.Testing.Platform, the `--logger` flag from `dotnet test` might not work the same way. The platform has its own command-line arguments. Let me check how to enable TRX reporting with Microsoft.Testing.Platform.

With Microsoft.Testing.Platform, you typically pass `--report-trx` directly to the test executable, or use the `--` separator with `dotnet test` to pass platform-specific arguments.

data.content: The project needs `EnableMSTestRunner` to build as an executable that supports the testing platform extensions directly. Let me update the project:
data.reasoningText:
OK, no TRX file anywhere. Let me look at this differently. The issue might be that with `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`, the MSTest runner needs to be explicitly enabled with `<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>` for the TRX report extension to work properly.

Let me look at what the MSTest package provides versus what `EnableMSTestRunner` adds. Actually, with MSTest 3.x, if you use the `MSTest` meta package (which includes MSTest.TestFramework, MSTest.TestAdapter, and MSTest.Analyzers), and you set `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport`, it should work. 

So the distinction is that `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` switches to the testing platform protocol, but `EnableMSTestRunner` is what actually builds the project as an executable with the testing platform entry point instead of a library. When only `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` is set, MSBuild still handles test execution and pipes the results, which might bypass the TRX extension entirely.

I should set both `EnableMSTestRunner` and `OutputType` to make this a proper standalone test executable that can register and use the TRX report extension through the testing platform pipeline.
